runs.eval_name: Last-to-leave format fatigue diagnosis and strategy
expectations: Identifies this as format fatigue / format saturation
Recommends not running same format back-to-back (minimum 2-3 other formats in between)
Suggests either resting the format temporarily or adding a significant twist
References the principle that viewers don't actually want the same format forever
Provides at least one concrete innovation idea for making the format feel fresh

notes: With-skill runs achieved perfect pass rates (1.0) on all 3 evals.
Without-skill runs averaged 0.80 pass rate, with the largest gap on eval 2 (format fatigue: 0.60 vs 1.0).

Eval 2 was the most discriminating: the skill provides two specific insights the baseline missed — the 2-3 format rotation rule (vs. generic 'space them out' advice) and the viewer stated-preference paradox (viewers say they want more but engagement drops anyway).
Eval 1 showed moderate separation: baseline missed the specific 'stair-stepping' named format but correctly applied the escalation principle.

Eval 3 showed no separation on pass rate (both 1.0), but qualitative analysis reveals the baseline fell into the day-by-day vlog structure trap that the skill explicitly warns against. The evals lack an assertion to catch this difference — a gap worth addressing.
The skill adds most value for format-naming precision and rotation rules. The open-loop/payoff principles appear to be general enough that a capable baseline model applies them naturally.
Recommendation: Add an assertion to eval 3 checking whether the response warns against chronological day-by-day structure to increase discriminating power.
